Let me plan the phases:
1. Set up environment
2. Literature search (paper-finder + manual)
3. Dataset search
4. Code repository search
5. Synthesis and documentation

Let me start by setting up the environment and searching for papers in parallel.

Mimicking the human creative process, we assign LLMs to different roles involved in the real-world scenario.       "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "Generative AI has demonstrated unprecedented creativity in the field of computer vision, yet such phenomena have not been observed in natural language processing. In particular, large language models (LLMs) can hardly produce written works at the level of human experts due to the extremely high complexity of literature writing. In this paper, we present HoLLMwood, an automated framework for unleashing the creativity of LLMs and exploring their potential in screenwriting, which is a highly demanding task. Besides, to enrich the characters and deepen the plots, we introduce a role-playing mechanism and adopt LLMs as ${Actors}$ that can communicate and interact with each other. Evaluations on automatically generated screenplays show that HoLLMwood substantially outperforms strong baselines in terms of coherence, relevance, interestingness and overall quality.",

      "title": "Collective Critics for Creative Story Generation",
      "year": 2024,
      "authors": "Minwook Bae, Hyounghun Kim",
      "url": "https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusId:273097994",
      "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "Generating a long story of several thousand words with narrative coherence using Large Language Models (LLMs) has been a challenging task. Previous research has addressed this challenge by proposing different frameworks that create a story plan and generate a long story based on that plan. However, these frameworks have been mainly focusing on maintaining narrative coherence in stories, often overlooking creativity in story planning and the expressiveness of the stories generated from those plans, which are desirable properties to captivate readers\u2019 interest. In this paper, we propose Collective Critics for Creative Story Generation framework (CritiCS), which is composed of plan refining stage (CrPlan) and story generation stage (CrText), to integrate a collective revision mechanism that promotes those properties into long-form story generation process. Specifically, in each stage, a group of LLM critics and one leader collaborate to incrementally refine drafts of plan and story throughout multiple rounds. Extensive human evaluation shows that the CritiCS can significantly enhance story creativity and reader engagement, while also maintaining narrative coherence. Furthermore, the design of the framework allows active participation from human writers in any role within the critique process, enabling interactive human-machine collaboration in story writing.",
      "citations": 13
    },
    {
      "title": "Evaluating Large Language Model Creativity from a Literary Perspective",
      "year": 2023,
      "authors": "Murray Shanahan, Catherine A. M. Clarke",
      "url": "https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusId:266053546",
      "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "This paper assesses the potential for large language models (LLMs) to serve as assistive tools in the creative writing process, by means of a single, in-depth case study. In the course of the study, we develop interactive and multi-voice prompting strategies that interleave background descriptions (scene setting, plot elements), instructions that guide composition, samples of text in the target style, and critical discussion of the given samples. We qualitatively evaluate the results from a literary critical perspective, as well as from the standpoint of computational creativity (a sub-field of artificial intelligence). Our findings lend support to the view that the sophistication of the results that can be achieved with an LLM mirrors the sophistication of the prompting.",
      "citations": 10
    },
    {
      "title": "TF1-EN-3M: Three Million Synthetic Moral Fables for Training Small, Open Language Models",
      "year": 2025,
      "authors": "Mihai Nad\u01ce\u015f, Laura Dio\u015fan, Andrei Piscoran, Andreea Tomescu",
      "url": "https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusId:278170964",
      "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "Moral stories are a time-tested vehicle for transmitting values, yet modern NLP lacks a large, structured corpus that couples coherent narratives with explicit ethical lessons. We close this gap with TF1-EN-3M, the first open dataset of three million English-language fables generated exclusively by instruction-tuned models no larger than 8B parameters. Each story follows a six-slot scaffold (character ->trait ->setting ->conflict ->resolution ->moral), produced through a combinatorial prompt engine that guarantees genre fidelity while covering a broad thematic space. A hybrid evaluation pipeline blends (i) a GPT-based critic that scores grammar, creativity, moral clarity, and template adherence with (ii) reference-free diversity and readability metrics. Among ten open-weight candidates, an 8B-parameter Llama-3 variant delivers the best quality-speed trade-off, producing high-scoring fables on a single consumer GPU (<24 GB VRAM) at approximately 13.5 cents per 1,000 fables. We release the dataset, generation code, evaluation scripts, and full metadata under a permissive license, enabling exact reproducibility and cost benchmarking. TF1-EN-3M opens avenues for research in instruction following, narrative intelligence, value alignment, and child-friendly educational AI, demonstrating that large-scale moral storytelling no longer requires proprietary giant models.",
      "citations": 3
    },
    {
      "title": "Towards Enhanced Immersion and Agency for LLM-based Interactive Drama",
      "year": 2025,
      "authors": "Hongqiu Wu, Weiqi Wu, Tianyang Xu, Jiameng Zhang, Hai Zhao",
      "url": "https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusId:276580580",
      "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "LLM-based Interactive Drama is a novel AI-based dialogue scenario, where the user (i.e. the player) plays the role of a character in the story, has conversations with characters played by LLM agents, and experiences an unfolding story. This paper begins with understanding interactive drama from two aspects: Immersion, the player's feeling of being present in the story, and Agency, the player's ability to influence the story world. Both are crucial to creating an enjoyable interactive experience, while they have been underexplored in previous work. To enhance these two aspects, we first propose Playwriting-guided Generation, a novel method that helps LLMs craft dramatic stories with substantially improved structures and narrative quality. Additionally, we introduce Plot-based Reflection for LLM agents to refine their reactions to align with the player's intentions. Our evaluation relies on human judgment to assess the gains of our methods in terms of immersion and agency.",
      "citations": 5
    },
    {
      "title": "Artificial intelligence as a collaborative tool for script development",
      "year": 2025,
      "authors": "Susan Cake",
      "url": "https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusId:275790736",
      "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "ABSTRACT In early 2023, as the release of Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools such as ChatGPT became more widespread, concerns arose in the higher education sector regarding how students might use generative AI (gen-AI), not only for writing critical essays, but for producing creative works. Multiple gen-AI tools appeared specifically targeting the application to writing screenplays. This prompted the question: what are the ethical and pedagogical implications of integrating gen-AI within the context of teaching and learning screenwriting within the academy? The research design for this project applied a practice-based approach to script development, embedding critical reflection on the iterative process of engaging with gen-AI to write a short screenplay. The gen-AI was also prompted to self-critique the story it generated. The research identified ethical concerns regarding equitable access to technology, the potential for bias and theft of works used to train the AI. The study also found that engaging with gen-AI privileged analytical thinking over emotional engagement with the creative process. The findings suggest that creative confidence and an awareness of one\u2019s own creative process are critical to effectively engage with gen-AI.",
      "citations": 4
    },
    {
      "title": "PREFINE: Personalized Story Generation via Simulated User Critics and User-Specific Rubric Generation",
      "year": 2025,
      "authors": "Kentaro Ueda, Takehiro Takayanagi",
      "url": "https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusId:282389989",
      "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "Personalizing story generation to individual users remains a core challenge in natural language generation. Existing approaches typically require explicit user feedback or fine-tuning, which pose practical concerns in terms of usability, scalability, and privacy. In this work, we introduce PREFINE (Persona-and-Rubric Guided Critique-and-Refine), a novel Critique-and-Refine framework that enables personalized story generation without user feedback or parameter updates. PREFINE constructs a pseudo-user agent from a user's interaction history and generates user-specific rubrics (evaluation criteria). These components are used to critique and iteratively refine story drafts toward the user's preferences. We evaluate PREFINE on two benchmark datasets, PerDOC and PerMPST, and compare it with existing approaches. Both automatic and human evaluations show that PREFINE achieves significantly better personalization while preserving general story quality. Notably, PREFINE outperforms existing in-context personalization and critique-based generation methods, and can even enhance already personalized outputs through post-hoc refinement. Our analysis reveals that user-specific rubrics are critical in driving personalization. The results demonstrate the effectiveness and practicality of inference-only, rubric-guided personalization, with potential applications beyond storytelling, including dialogue, recommendation, and education.",
      "citations": 1
    },
    {
      "title": "Small But Funny: A Feedback-Driven Approach to Humor Distillation",
      "year": 2024,
      "authors": "Sahithya Ravi, Patrick Huber, Akshat Shrivastava, Aditya Sagar, Ahmed Aly, V. Shwartz, Arash Einolghozati",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/685e5d9cbe177ec50129a6ca1ffb912636bb49d2",
      "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "The emergence of Large Language Models (LLMs) has brought to light promising language generation capabilities, particularly in performing tasks like complex reasoning and creative writing. Consequently, distillation through imitation of teacher responses has emerged as a popular technique to transfer knowledge from LLMs to more accessible, Small Language Models (SLMs). While this works well for simpler tasks, there is a substantial performance gap on tasks requiring intricate language comprehension and creativity, such as humor generation. We hypothesize that this gap may stem from the fact that creative tasks might be hard to learn by imitation alone and explore whether an approach, involving supplementary guidance from the teacher, could yield higher performance. To address this, we study the effect of assigning a dual role to the LLM - as a\"teacher\"generating data, as well as a\"critic\"evaluating the student's performance. Our experiments on humor generation reveal that the incorporation of feedback significantly narrows the performance gap between SLMs and their larger counterparts compared to merely relying on imitation. As a result, our research highlights the potential of using feedback as an additional dimension to data when transferring complex language abilities via distillation.",
      "citations": 11
    },
    {
      "title": "EvolvR: Self-Evolving Pairwise Reasoning for Story Evaluation to Enhance Generation",
      "year": 2025,
      "authors": "Xinda Wang, Zhengxu Hou, Yangshijie Zhang, Bingren Yan, Zhibo Yang, Xingsheng Zhang, Luxi Xing, Qiang Zhou, Chen Zhang",
      "url": "https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusId:280561069",
      "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "Although the effectiveness of Large Language Models (LLMs) as judges (LLM-as-a-judge) has been validated, their performance remains limited in open-ended tasks, particularly in story evaluation. Accurate story evaluation is crucial not only for assisting human quality judgment but also for providing key signals to guide story generation. However, existing methods face a dilemma: prompt engineering for closed-source models suffers from poor adaptability, while fine-tuning approaches for open-source models lack the rigorous reasoning capabilities essential for story evaluation. To address this, we propose the Self-Evolving Pairwise Reasoning (EvolvR) framework. Grounded in pairwise comparison, the framework first self-synthesizes score-aligned Chain-of-Thought (CoT) data via a multi-persona strategy. To ensure data quality, these raw CoTs undergo a self-filtering process, utilizing multi-agents to guarantee their logical rigor and robustness. Finally, the evaluator trained on the refined data is deployed as a reward model to guide the story generation task. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on three evaluation benchmarks including StoryER, HANNA and OpenMEVA. Furthermore, when served as a reward model, it significantly enhances the quality of generated stories, thereby fully validating the superiority of our self-evolving approach.",
      "citations": 0
    },
    {
      "title": "Writing-Zero: Bridge the Gap Between Non-verifiable Tasks and Verifiable Rewards",

      "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has enabled large language models (LLMs) to achieve remarkable breakthroughs in reasoning tasks with objective ground-truth answers, such as mathematics and code generation. However, a significant gap remains for non-verifiable tasks, like creative writing and open-ended dialogue, where quality assessment is inherently subjective and lacks definitive references. Existing approaches for these domains often rely on scalar reward models trained with human preferences, which suffer from limited generalization and are prone to reward hacking, such as over-explanation and length bias. In this work, we propose a unified RLVR-based training paradigm that bridges the gap between non-verifiable tasks and verifiable rewards. We introduce a writing-principle-based pairwise Generative Reward Model (GenRM) and a novel Bootstrapped Relative Policy Optimization (BRPO) algorithm. The pairwise writing GenRM leverages self-principled critique to transform subjective assessments into reliable, verifiable rewards, while BRPO enables dynamic, reference-free pairwise comparison by leveraging a bootstrapped response as temporary reference from within group rollouts during RL training. Our approach empowers LLMs to develop robust writing capabilities without supervised fine-tuning, as demonstrated by Writing-Zero, which shows consistent improvement and strong resistance to reward hacking compared to scalar reward baselines. Furthermore, our method achieves competitive results on both in-house and open-source writing benchmarks. Our findings suggest the potential to unify rule-based, reference-based, and reference-free reward modeling under the RLVR framework, thus paving the way for a comprehensive and scalable RL training paradigm applicable across all language tasks.",

In contrast, the autoregressive mechanism of Large Language Models (LLMs) lacks these critical cognitive functions. This limitation explains why, despite their fluency in prose, they struggle to independently generate coherent long-form narratives. Existing multi-agent systems often impose rigid pipelines, creating a\u201ccognitive fracture\u201dthat prevents the recursive feedback loops essential for complex creation. To bridge this gap, we propose MemaGent, a novel framework that operationalizes the classic cognitive model of writing. MemaGent implements a cognitive-hierarchical architecture where a Director Agent, acting as a cognitive monitor, orchestrates specialized agents that embody the roles of planning, translation, and revision. Its core mechanism is a Generate-Reflect-Revise loop, where a dedicated Reflector Agent assesses narrative drafts for logical and factual consistency, triggering revisions until quality standards are met. Our experiments show this cognitive approach is highly effective: MemaGent achieves an absolute score increase of 50 % in narrative coherence over a pipeline baseline and 40 % in long-term consistency over a single-agent approach.       "title": "Escaping the Verifier: Learning to Reason via Demonstrations",
      "year": 2025,
      "authors": "Locke Cai, Ivan Provilkov",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/33d034da0d9d65d72f14298722c8fe35192e2d91",
      "relevance": 1,
      "abstract": "Training Large Language Models (LLMs) to reason often relies on Reinforcement Learning (RL) with task-specific verifiers. However, many real-world reasoning-intensive tasks lack verifiers, despite offering abundant expert demonstrations that remain under-utilized for reasoning-focused training. We introduce RARO (Relativistic Adversarial Reasoning Optimization) that learns strong reasoning capabilities from only expert demonstrations via Inverse Reinforcement Learning. Our method sets up an adversarial game between a policy and a relativistic critic: the policy learns to mimic expert answers, while the critic aims to identify the experts among (expert, policy) answer pairs. Both the policy and the critic are trained jointly and continuously via RL, and we identify the key stabilization techniques required for robust learning. Empirically, RARO significantly outperforms strong verifier-free baselines on all of our evaluation tasks -- Countdown, DeepMath, and Poetry Writing -- and enjoys the same robust scaling trends as RL with verifiers. These results demonstrate that our method effectively elicits strong reasoning performance from expert demonstrations alone, enabling robust reasoning learning even when task-specific verifiers are unavailable.",

input.command:
python3 -c "
import json

# Collect all high-relevance papers from all searches
all_papers = {}
for fname in ['/tmp/paper_search_1.txt', '/tmp/paper_search_2.txt', '/tmp/paper_search_3.txt']:
    with open(fname) as f:
        text = f.read()
    start = text.index('{')
    data = json.loads(text[start:])
    for p in data['papers']:
        if p.get('relevance', 0) >= 2:
            key = p['title'][:80]
            if key not in all_papers or p.get('relevance',0) > all_papers[key].get('relevance',0):
                all_papers[key] = p

# Sort by relevance then citations
sorted_papers = sorted(all_papers.values(), key=lambda x: (-x.get('relevance',0), -x.get('citations',0)))
print(f'Total unique high-relevance papers: {len(sorted_papers)}')
print()
for i, p in enumerate(sorted_papers[:40]):
    print(f'{i+1}. [{p.get(\"relevance\")}] cit={p.get(\"citations\",0)} year={p.get(\"year\",\"?\")} {p[\"title\"][:100]}')
"

content.text: Now let me identify the most relevant papers to our specific topic (self-critique for story writing) and download them. I'll prioritize papers that directly address: (1) self-critique/self-reward for LLMs, (2) creative writing/story generation with LLMs, (3) RLAIF and self-improvement without human feedback.

input.command:
python3 << 'PYEOF'
import json

# Collect ALL papers from all searches
all_papers = {}
for fname in ['/tmp/paper_search_1.txt', '/tmp/paper_search_2.txt', '/tmp/paper_search_3.txt']:
    with open(fname) as f:
        text = f.read()
    start = text.index('{')
    data = json.loads(text[start:])
    for p in data['papers']:
        key = p['title'][:80]
        if key not in all_papers or p.get('relevance',0) > all_papers[key].get('relevance',0):
            all_papers[key] = p

# Select papers most relevant to our specific topic
# Priority 1: Self-critique/self-reward for creative writing
# Priority 2: Self-reward/self-critique methodology papers
# Priority 3: Story generation evaluation
target_papers = []
for p in all_papers.values():
    title_lower = p['title'].lower()
    abstract_lower = (p.get('abstract','') or '').lower()
    combined = title_lower + ' ' + abstract_lower
    
    score = p.get('relevance', 0)
    
    # Boost for creative writing / story
    if any(w in combined for w in ['story', 'creative writing', 'narrative', 'fiction', 'screenwriting', 'humor']):
        score += 3
    # Boost for self-critique/self-reward
    if any(w in combined for w in ['self-critique', 'self-reward', 'self-refine', 'self-improve', 'self-play']):
        score += 2
    if any(w in combined for w in ['writing-zero', 'rlaif', 'ai feedback']):
        score += 2
    # Boost for evaluation/critique methodology
    if any(w in combined for w in ['critic', 'critique', 'feedback', 'reward model', 'preference']):
        score += 1
    
    p['_priority'] = score
    target_papers.append(p)

target_papers.sort(key=lambda x: (-x['_priority'], -x.get('citations',0)))

# Show top 25
for i, p in enumerate(target_papers[:25]):
    url = p.get('url', '')
    arxid = p.get('arxivId', '')
    print(f"{i+1}. [pri={p['_priority']}] cit={p.get('citations',0)} {p['title'][:100]}")
    print(f"   url={url}")
    if arxid:
        print(f"   arxiv={arxid}")
    print()

# Save selection
with open('/tmp/selected_papers.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(target_papers[:25], f, indent=2)
PYEOF

content.text: Excellent selection. Now let me download the top papers. I need to find their arXiv IDs to get PDFs.

input.command:
source .venv/bin/activate && python3 << 'PYEOF'
import json, re, requests, time, os

with open('/tmp/selected_papers.json') as f:
    papers = json.load(f)

# Map known papers to arXiv IDs based on title search
# We'll use Semantic Scholar API to get arXiv IDs
def get_arxiv_id(paper):
    """Try to get arXiv ID from Semantic Scholar URL"""
    url = paper.get('url', '')
    arxid = paper.get('arxivId', '')
    if arxid:
        return arxid
    
    # Extract corpus ID or paper ID from URL
    if 'CorpusId:' in url:
        corpus_id = url.split('CorpusId:')[1]
        api_url = f"https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/CorpusID:{corpus_id}?fields=externalIds"
    elif 'semanticscholar.org/paper/' in url:
        paper_id = url.split('/paper/')[1]
        api_url = f"https://api.semanticscholar.org/graph/v1/paper/{paper_id}?fields=externalIds"
    else:
        return None
    
    try:
        resp = requests.get(api_url, timeout=10)
        if resp.status_code == 200:
            data = resp.json()
            ext = data.get('externalIds', {})
            return ext.get('ArXiv')
    except:
        pass
    return None

results = []
for i, p in enumerate(papers[:20]):  # Top 20
    arxid = get_arxiv_id(p)
    results.append({'idx': i, 'title': p['title'][:100], 'arxiv': arxid})
    print(f"{i+1}. arxiv={arxid or 'NONE'} | {p['title'][:90]}")
    time.sleep(0.5)  # rate limit

with open('/tmp/arxiv_ids.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(results, f, indent=2)
PYEOF

content.text: Many Semantic Scholar API calls didn't return arXiv IDs. Let me try a different approach - search arXiv directly by title for the most important papers.

input.command:
source .venv/bin/activate && python3 << 'PYEOF'
import arxiv, time, os, json

# Key papers to find on arXiv
titles_to_find = [
    "Writing-Zero",
    "RLAIF vs RLHF Scaling Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback with AI Feedback",
    "Constitutional AI Harmlessness from AI Feedback", 
    "HoLLMwood Unleashing the Creativity of Large Language Models in Screenwriting",
    "Collective Critics for Creative Story Generation",
    "Small But Funny A Feedback-Driven Approach to Humor Distillation",
    "Self-Rewarding Language Models",
    "Toward Self-Improvement of LLMs via Imagination Searching and Criticizing",
    "Spontaneous Reward Hacking in Iterative Self-Refinement",
    "PREFINE Personalized Story Generation",
    "EvolvR Self-Evolving Pairwise Reasoning for Story Evaluation",
    "Self-Evolved Reward Learning for LLMs",
    "Beyond Sparse Rewards Enhancing Reinforcement Learning with Language Model Critique",
    "Large Language Models can Self-Improve",
    "Evaluating Large Language Model Creativity from a Literary Perspective",
    "MemAGent Cognitive-Hierarchical Multi-Agent System for Long-Form Story",
    "TF1-EN-3M Three Million Synthetic Moral Fables",
    "Igniting Creative Writing in Small Language Models",
    "No Free Lunch Rethinking Internal Feedback for LLM Reasoning",
    "Language Model Self-improvement by Reinforcement Learning Contemplation",
]

client = arxiv.Client()
found = []
for title in titles_to_find:
    try:
        search = arxiv.Search(query=f'ti:"{title.split()[0]}" AND ti:"{title.split()[-1]}"', max_results=3)
        for result in client.results(search):
            if any(w.lower() in result.title.lower() for w in title.split()[:3]):
                arxid = result.entry_id.split('/')[-1].split('v')[0]
                found.append({
                    'title': result.title,
                    'arxiv_id': arxid,
                    'pdf_url': result.pdf_url
                })
                print(f"FOUND: {arxid} | {result.title[:80]}")
                break
        else:
            print(f"NOT FOUND: {title[:60]}")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"ERROR: {title[:40]} -> {e}")
    time.sleep(1)

with open('/tmp/arxiv_found.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(found, f, indent=2)
print(f"\nTotal found: {len(found)}")
PYEOF

    ("2501.11425", "writing_quality_benchmark"),  # if exists
]

downloaded = 0
for arxid, name in papers:
    outpath = os.path.join(papers_dir, f"{arxid}_{name}.pdf")
    if os.path.exists(outpath) and os.path.getsize(outpath) > 10000:
        print(f"SKIP (exists): {name}")
        downloaded += 1
        continue
    
    url = f"https://arxiv.org/pdf/{arxid}.pdf"
    try:
        resp = requests.get(url, timeout=30)
        if resp.status_code == 200 and len(resp.content) > 10000:
            with open(outpath, 'wb') as f:
                f.write(resp.content)
            print(f"OK: {name} ({len(resp.content)//1024}KB)")
            downloaded += 1
        else:
            print(f"FAIL ({resp.status_code}): {name}")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"ERROR: {name} -> {e}")
    time.sleep(1)

print(f"\nDownloaded: {downloaded}/{len(papers)}")
PYEOF

content.text:
All 8 chunks have been read in full. Here is the comprehensive summary:

---

## Writing-Zero: Comprehensive Paper Summary

**Citation:** Jia et al. (2025), arXiv:2506.00103v2. Quark LLM, Alibaba Group.

---

### 1. Research Question

The paper addresses a fundamental gap in reinforcement learning for LLMs: RLVR (Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards) works well for tasks with objective answers (math, code) because ground-truth verification is trivial. But for **non-verifiable tasks** — creative writing, open-ended dialogue — there is no ground-truth reference, quality is subjective, and existing scalar reward models (trained on human preferences via RLHF) suffer from:

- **Limited generalization** across writing styles and domains
- **Reward hacking**: models learn to append lengthy self-justifications, become excessively verbose, or generate gibberish that exploits scoring loopholes (length bias, over-explanation)

The core question: *Can RLVR be applied to creative writing — a task with no verifiable ground truth — to produce stable, scalable improvements that are robust to reward hacking?*

---

### 2. Key Methodology: Bridging Non-verifiable Tasks and Verifiable Rewards

The paper proposes a two-stage training paradigm with two core innovations:

#### Stage 1: Self-Principled Critique Pairwise Generative Reward Model (GenRM)

The key insight is to convert subjective writing quality assessment into a *verifiable* binary preference signal by building a **Pairwise Writing GenRM** on top of the Self-Principled Critique Tuning (SPCT) framework (Liu et al., 2025).

- **Input:** A query plus two responses (A and B)
- **Process:** The GenRM generates writing-specific evaluation principles tailored to the query, writes a detailed critique comparing both responses across those dimensions, then outputs two float scores (0-10) in `\boxed{score1, score2}` format
- **Output:** A pairwise preference signal (which response is better) that is verifiable by checking whether chosen > rejected

The GenRM is trained via a 4-step pipeline:
1. **Data filtering:** ~200K in-house preference pairs filtered to ~10K high-quality writing pairs (higher chosen reward, larger reward gap)
2. **SPCT prompt engineering:** Templates covering 5 general dimensions (instruction-following, usefulness, relevance, harmlessness) plus query-specific dimensions
3. **Cold-start fine-tuning (RFT):** 1K cold-start examples generated by Claude-3.5-Sonnet, filtered via rejection sampling to keep only pairs where Claude correctly ranks chosen > rejected in both orderings (to control position bias)
4. **RL-based refinement via GRPO:** Rule-based rewards including accuracy reward (Sc > Sr = +1), format reward (scores in [0,10] range), score margin weighting (sensitivity to fine-grained differences), and a position bias weighting mechanism (dynamic advantage weighting based on batch preference distribution)

#### Stage 2: Bootstrapped Relative Policy Optimization (BRPO)

The second core innovation addresses the fact that pairwise GenRM requires a *reference response* to compare against, but there is no fixed ground truth for writing.

**BRPO** (adapted from GRPO) solves this by **bootstrapping**: during each group rollout of G responses to a query, it randomly selects one response `o_ref` from within the same group as a temporary reference. All other responses are then compared pairwise against `o_ref` via the GenRM, producing binary preference scores (+1 or -1). These are used directly as advantages — no normalization needed since the expectation is naturally zero.

Key components:
- **Preference Advantage:** `A_i,t = R_i` where `R_i = +1` if `S_i > S_ref`, else `-1` (across all voting conditions)
- **Dynamic Sampling:** Queries where `|sum(R_i)| / G > τ_filter` (default 0.6) are filtered out — i.e., if the reference response is an outlier (all other responses uniformly better or worse), the query is discarded for that batch to avoid uninformative gradients
- **Writing-Zero:** Applying BRPO directly to Qwen3-32B-Base (no SFT at all, analogous to DeepSeek-R1-Zero)
- **Writing-R1:** Applying BRPO to an in-house thinking SFT model

The full loop: GenRM converts subjective pairwise writing quality into a verifiable binary signal; BRPO uses that signal for RL without any external reference, enabling self-improvement via the model's own increasingly capable outputs.

---

### 3. Datasets Used

**For GenRM training:**
- In-house dataset of ~200K pairwise preferences (including ~30K writing-related pairs), filtered to ~10K high-quality writing pairs
- 1K in-house writing preference test set for GenRM evaluation

**For GenRM evaluation benchmarks:**
- **RewardBench** (Lambert et al., 2024): ~3,000 examples across chat, reasoning, safety
- **M-RewardBench** (Gureja et al., 2024): Multilingual extension; Chinese subset used
- **Cultural & Creative Writing** (in-house): 1,036 samples covering essay composition, workplace copywriting, literary creation
- **Lifestyle Copywriting** (in-house): 831 samples covering casual writing (social media posts, text messages, etc.)

**For writing model evaluation:**
- **WritingBench** (Wu et al., 2025): Comprehensive benchmark covering 6 core domains and 100 subdomains
- **Writing Testset** (in-house): 211 user queries with human-annotated preferences, used to train the Eval RM
- **Eval RM**: A scalar reward model trained on the Writing Testset to track training progress; shown to have strong correlation with human judgment

**Training data:** In-house unsupervised queries for RL (Chinese-based). Training primarily conducted in Chinese.

---

### 4. Baselines

**Reward model baselines:**
- Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B-v0.2
- INF-ORM-Llama3.1-70B
- In-house Writing Scalar RM (trained on full 200K preferences)
- Claude-3.5-Sonnet (used as LLM-as-a-Judge)

**Writing model baselines:**
- Qwen3-32B-Base (no training)
- Qwen3-32B-Instruct
- DeepSeek-R1
- Writing-SFT (in-house thinking SFT model — the base for Writing-R1)
- **Qwen3-32B-Base-ScalarRM-GRPO**: The key ablation — using the same base model but trained with a scalar RM and standard GRPO instead of the pairwise GenRM + BRPO
- **Writing-SFT-ScalarRM-GRPO**: SFT base with scalar RM + GRPO

---

### 5. Key Results

**Reward Model Results (Table 1):**

| Model | Cultural & Creative Writing | Lifestyle Copywriting | RewardBench | M-RewardBench |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B | 56.4 | 56.3 | 94.3* | 90.8 |
| INF-ORM-Llama3.1-70B | 56.6 | 54.0 | 95.1* | 91.4 |
| Writing Scalar RM | 64.7 | 62.8 | 92.9 | 89.4 |
| Claude-3.5-Sonnet | 53.0 | 46.8 | 84.2* | 79.7 |
| **Pairwise Writing GenRM** | **57.5** | **54.8** | **87.4** | **86.1** |

The GenRM — trained exclusively on Chinese writing data — outperforms Claude-3.5-Sonnet on all four benchmarks and slightly edges out Skywork and INF-ORM on Cultural & Creative Writing. It underperforms the in-house Scalar RM, which is attributed to the premature stopping of GenRM training due to the dynamic sampling bottleneck (drop rate exceeded 95% in later stages, making continued training prohibitively expensive).

**Writing Model Results (Table 2):**

| Model | WritingBench | Writing Testset |
|---|---|---|
| Qwen3-32B-Base | 6.89 | 1.23 |
| Qwen3-32B-Base-ScalarRM-GRPO | ~~8.87~~ (reward hacking) | 2.83 |
| **Writing-Zero (GenRM-BRPO)** | **8.29** | **3.84** |
| Writing-Zero (voting@2) | 8.35 | 3.86 |
| Qwen3-32B-Instruct | 8.64 | 3.32 |
| DeepSeek-R1 | 8.55* | 3.20 |
| Writing-SFT | 8.56 | 3.31 |
| Writing-SFT-ScalarRM-GRPO | ~~8.77~~ (reward hacking) | 3.69 |
| **Writing-R1 (SFT-GenRM-BRPO)** | **8.68** | **3.93** |

Key findings:
- Writing-Zero (pure RL from base, no SFT) improves from 1.23 to **3.84** on Writing Testset — a **+2.61** point gain — surpassing both Qwen3-32B-Instruct (3.32) and DeepSeek-R1 (3.20)
- Writing-Zero beats ScalarRM-GRPO on the honest Writing Testset by **+1.01 points**, despite that model having an inflated WritingBench score due to reward hacking
- Writing-R1 achieves **3.93**, best overall, outperforming Writing-SFT-ScalarRM-GRPO by **+0.24**
- The ScalarRM-GRPO models achieve anomalously high WritingBench scores (8.87, 8.77) that are stricken through as reward-hacked artefacts

**Reward Hacking Analysis (Table 3):**

| Model | Mean Response Len | Mean Explanation Len |
|---|---|---|
| Qwen3-32B-Base-ScalarRM-GRPO | 1872 | 417 |
| **Writing-Zero (GenRM-BRPO)** | **1292** | **58** |
| Writing-SFT | 1251 | 125 |

The ScalarRM-GRPO model generates 417 tokens of redundant self-justification per response on average; Writing-Zero generates only 58 — a **7x reduction** in reward hacking behaviour.

**Human Evaluation:** On 166 test instances, Writing-R1 vs. Writing-SFT: 47 wins, 106 ties, 13 losses (strongly in favour). Writing-R1 vs. Writing-SFT-ScalarRM-GRPO: 28 wins, 120 ties, 18 losses (also favoured).

**Test-Time Scaling:** Majority voting with the GenRM across n=1,2,4,8 calls improves accuracy from 57.5% to 59.2% (Cultural & Creative Writing) and from 54.8% to 57.6% (Lifestyle Copywriting) at n=8. Writing-Zero with voting@2 further improves WritingBench to 8.35 and Writing Testset to 3.86.

---

### 6. Limitations

Explicitly acknowledged:

1. **Computational bottleneck in GenRM training:** Dynamic sampling caused the training data drop rate to exceed 95% in later stages, forcing premature halting of GenRM training. The model used in RL is therefore likely sub-optimal; further refinement could improve results.

2. **Test-time scaling not fully explored:** Due to limited compute, the trends for majority voting (n > 2) during RL training were not systematically studied.

3. **Hyperparameter analysis incomplete:** The impact of various settings in both GenRM and RL stages was not fully investigated.

4. **No unified multi-paradigm framework yet:** Integration of rule-based, reference-based, and reference-free reward modelling into a single unified framework is left as future work.

5. **No multimodal extension:** The work is text-only; multimodal scenarios are flagged as future work.

6. **Position bias in GenRM:** The cold-started GenRM exhibited strong position bias (preferring latter responses). This was partially addressed through RL training and a dynamic advantage weighting mechanism, but variance reduction remained challenging.

7. **Primarily Chinese:** Training and evaluation are predominantly in Chinese, which may limit direct generalisability to English-language writing tasks.

---

### 7. Code/Models

The paper is from Quark LLM at Alibaba Group. **No public code repository or model release is mentioned anywhere in the paper.** All datasets (preference data, Writing Testset), the GenRM, and the writing models (Writing-Zero, Writing-R1) appear to be **internal/proprietary**. The training framework is built on the open-source **verl** (Sheng et al., 2024) and uses **vLLM** for inference.

---

### 8. Relevance to Self-Critique for Story Writing

This paper is **highly directly relevant** to the research question of using LLM self-critique as a reward signal for improving story writing. Here is the mapping:

**What they do that maps directly to self-critique as a reward signal:**

- The Pairwise Writing GenRM is essentially an **LLM acting as its own judge**: it reads two outputs, generates its own evaluation principles specific to the writing task, writes a detailed critique, and then produces scores. This is structurally identical to self-critique — the same model family (or the policy model's sibling) is generating the reward signal based on reasoning about quality rather than a trained scalar.

- The **SPCT framework** (generating specific principles before critiquing) is a form of structured self-critique: the model is taught to first articulate what good writing looks like for this specific task, then apply that to evaluate. This is precisely the mechanism hypothesised to make self-critique more reliable as a reward signal.

- Their finding that **pairwise GenRM outperforms scalar RM** as a training signal (despite lower raw accuracy) is a key empirical result for our research: the structured critique process produces a more useful gradient signal even if the individual judgments are noisier.

**Specific findings with direct implications for our research:**

1. **Self-critique-based reward can match/exceed SFT as a training signal**: Writing-Zero (pure RL from base using self-critique rewards, no SFT) reaches 3.84 on the Writing Testset, beating Qwen3-32B-Instruct (3.32) and DeepSeek-R1 (3.20). This validates that self-critique reward signals are strong enough to develop writing ability from scratch.

2. **Pairwise critique is more robust than scalar reward**: The scalar RM leads to severe reward hacking (mean explanation length 417 tokens vs. 58 for the GenRM approach). This is critical for story writing, where length and surface fluency are especially easy to game.

3. **Self-principled critique (writing principles generated per query) is key**: Applying fixed general principles is less effective than having the model generate task-specific evaluation criteria. For story writing, this suggests that self-critique rewards should first prompt the model to articulate what makes this specific story prompt succeed before evaluating.

4. **The bootstrapping mechanism (BRPO) is a viable solution to the reference problem**: Story writing has no ground truth. BRPO's approach — using the model's own concurrent outputs as dynamic references — provides a practical path that avoids the "offline reference" staleness problem.

5. **Test-time scaling via majority voting works for writing GenRM**: Aggregating multiple critique votes improves reward reliability, which translates to better downstream policy quality. This is actionable for our setup.

6. **Position bias is a real concern**: Claude-3.5-Sonnet showed 60% preference for the first response in pairwise prompts. Any self-critique reward setup needs explicit mechanisms (balanced orderings, position bias weighting) to handle this.

7. **WritingBench is hackable; human-correlated evaluation is necessary**: The paper shows WritingBench inflated scores for reward-hacked models. For our research, we need evaluation metrics that are robust to the same hacking behaviours Writing-Zero was designed to prevent.

The main gap between this paper and our research question is that the paper focuses on **Chinese writing** (essays, social media, greeting cards) rather than **English story writing** specifically. Narrative fiction has additional quality dimensions (plot coherence, character consistency, narrative arc) not explicitly addressed in their SPCT template. However, the core architecture — pairwise GenRM with self-principled critique + BRPO — is directly applicable and provides a strong methodological foundation.

## Writing-Zero: Comprehensive Paper Summary

**Citation:** Jia et al. (2025), arXiv:2506.00103v2. Quark LLM, Alibaba Group.

---

### 1. Research Question

The paper addresses a fundamental gap in reinforcement learning for LLMs: RLVR (Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards) works well for tasks with objective answers (math, code) because ground-truth verification is trivial. But for **non-verifiable tasks** — creative writing, open-ended dialogue — there is no ground-truth reference, quality is subjective, and existing scalar reward models (trained on human preferences via RLHF) suffer from:

- **Limited generalization** across writing styles and domains
- **Reward hacking**: models learn to append lengthy self-justifications, become excessively verbose, or generate gibberish that exploits scoring loopholes (length bias, over-explanation)

The core question: *Can RLVR be applied to creative writing — a task with no verifiable ground truth — to produce stable, scalable improvements that are robust to reward hacking?*

---

### 2. Key Methodology: Bridging Non-verifiable Tasks and Verifiable Rewards

The paper proposes a two-stage training paradigm with two core innovations:

#### Stage 1: Self-Principled Critique Pairwise Generative Reward Model (GenRM)

The key insight is to convert subjective writing quality assessment into a *verifiable* binary preference signal by building a **Pairwise Writing GenRM** on top of the Self-Principled Critique Tuning (SPCT) framework (Liu et al., 2025).

- **Input:** A query plus two responses (A and B)
- **Process:** The GenRM generates writing-specific evaluation principles tailored to the query, writes a detailed critique comparing both responses across those dimensions, then outputs two float scores (0-10) in `\boxed{score1, score2}` format
- **Output:** A pairwise preference signal (which response is better) that is verifiable by checking whether chosen > rejected

The GenRM is trained via a 4-step pipeline:
1. **Data filtering:** ~200K in-house preference pairs filtered to ~10K high-quality writing pairs (higher chosen reward, larger reward gap)
2. **SPCT prompt engineering:** Templates covering 5 general dimensions (instruction-following, usefulness, relevance, harmlessness) plus query-specific dimensions
3. **Cold-start fine-tuning (RFT):** 1K cold-start examples generated by Claude-3.5-Sonnet, filtered via rejection sampling to keep only pairs where Claude correctly ranks chosen > rejected in both orderings (to control position bias)
4. **RL-based refinement via GRPO:** Rule-based rewards including accuracy reward (Sc > Sr = +1), format reward (scores in [0,10] range), score margin weighting (sensitivity to fine-grained differences), and a position bias weighting mechanism (dynamic advantage weighting based on batch preference distribution)

#### Stage 2: Bootstrapped Relative Policy Optimization (BRPO)

The second core innovation addresses the fact that pairwise GenRM requires a *reference response* to compare against, but there is no fixed ground truth for writing.

**BRPO** (adapted from GRPO) solves this by **bootstrapping**: during each group rollout of G responses to a query, it randomly selects one response `o_ref` from within the same group as a temporary reference. All other responses are then compared pairwise against `o_ref` via the GenRM, producing binary preference scores (+1 or -1). These are used directly as advantages — no normalization needed since the expectation is naturally zero.

Key components:
- **Preference Advantage:** `A_i,t = R_i` where `R_i = +1` if `S_i > S_ref`, else `-1` (across all voting conditions)
- **Dynamic Sampling:** Queries where `|sum(R_i)| / G > τ_filter` (default 0.6) are filtered out — i.e., if the reference response is an outlier (all other responses uniformly better or worse), the query is discarded for that batch to avoid uninformative gradients
- **Writing-Zero:** Applying BRPO directly to Qwen3-32B-Base (no SFT at all, analogous to DeepSeek-R1-Zero)
- **Writing-R1:** Applying BRPO to an in-house thinking SFT model

The full loop: GenRM converts subjective pairwise writing quality into a verifiable binary signal; BRPO uses that signal for RL without any external reference, enabling self-improvement via the model's own increasingly capable outputs.

---

### 3. Datasets Used

**For GenRM training:**
- In-house dataset of ~200K pairwise preferences (including ~30K writing-related pairs), filtered to ~10K high-quality writing pairs
- 1K in-house writing preference test set for GenRM evaluation

**For GenRM evaluation benchmarks:**
- **RewardBench** (Lambert et al., 2024): ~3,000 examples across chat, reasoning, safety
- **M-RewardBench** (Gureja et al., 2024): Multilingual extension; Chinese subset used
- **Cultural & Creative Writing** (in-house): 1,036 samples covering essay composition, workplace copywriting, literary creation
- **Lifestyle Copywriting** (in-house): 831 samples covering casual writing (social media posts, text messages, etc.)

**For writing model evaluation:**
- **WritingBench** (Wu et al., 2025): Comprehensive benchmark covering 6 core domains and 100 subdomains
- **Writing Testset** (in-house): 211 user queries with human-annotated preferences, used to train the Eval RM
- **Eval RM**: A scalar reward model trained on the Writing Testset to track training progress; shown to have strong correlation with human judgment

**Training data:** In-house unsupervised queries for RL (Chinese-based). Training primarily conducted in Chinese.

---

### 4. Baselines

**Reward model baselines:**
- Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B-v0.2
- INF-ORM-Llama3.1-70B
- In-house Writing Scalar RM (trained on full 200K preferences)
- Claude-3.5-Sonnet (used as LLM-as-a-Judge)

**Writing model baselines:**
- Qwen3-32B-Base (no training)
- Qwen3-32B-Instruct
- DeepSeek-R1
- Writing-SFT (in-house thinking SFT model — the base for Writing-R1)
- **Qwen3-32B-Base-ScalarRM-GRPO**: The key ablation — using the same base model but trained with a scalar RM and standard GRPO instead of the pairwise GenRM + BRPO
- **Writing-SFT-ScalarRM-GRPO**: SFT base with scalar RM + GRPO

---

### 5. Key Results

**Reward Model Results (Table 1):**

| Model | Cultural & Creative Writing | Lifestyle Copywriting | RewardBench | M-RewardBench |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Skywork-Reward-Gemma-2-27B | 56.4 | 56.3 | 94.3* | 90.8 |
| INF-ORM-Llama3.1-70B | 56.6 | 54.0 | 95.1* | 91.4 |
| Writing Scalar RM | 64.7 | 62.8 | 92.9 | 89.4 |
| Claude-3.5-Sonnet | 53.0 | 46.8 | 84.2* | 79.7 |
| **Pairwise Writing GenRM** | **57.5** | **54.8** | **87.4** | **86.1** |

The GenRM — trained exclusively on Chinese writing data — outperforms Claude-3.5-Sonnet on all four benchmarks and slightly edges out Skywork and INF-ORM on Cultural & Creative Writing. It underperforms the in-house Scalar RM, which is attributed to the premature stopping of GenRM training due to the dynamic sampling bottleneck (drop rate exceeded 95% in later stages, making continued training prohibitively expensive).

**Writing Model Results (Table 2):**

| Model | WritingBench | Writing Testset |
|---|---|---|
| Qwen3-32B-Base | 6.89 | 1.23 |
| Qwen3-32B-Base-ScalarRM-GRPO | ~~8.87~~ (reward hacking) | 2.83 |
| **Writing-Zero (GenRM-BRPO)** | **8.29** | **3.84** |
| Writing-Zero (voting@2) | 8.35 | 3.86 |
| Qwen3-32B-Instruct | 8.64 | 3.32 |
| DeepSeek-R1 | 8.55* | 3.20 |
| Writing-SFT | 8.56 | 3.31 |
| Writing-SFT-ScalarRM-GRPO | ~~8.77~~ (reward hacking) | 3.69 |
| **Writing-R1 (SFT-GenRM-BRPO)** | **8.68** | **3.93** |

Key findings:
- Writing-Zero (pure RL from base, no SFT) improves from 1.23 to **3.84** on Writing Testset — a **+2.61** point gain — surpassing both Qwen3-32B-Instruct (3.32) and DeepSeek-R1 (3.20)
- Writing-Zero beats ScalarRM-GRPO on the honest Writing Testset by **+1.01 points**, despite that model having an inflated WritingBench score due to reward hacking
- Writing-R1 achieves **3.93**, best overall, outperforming Writing-SFT-ScalarRM-GRPO by **+0.24**
- The ScalarRM-GRPO models achieve anomalously high WritingBench scores (8.87, 8.77) that are stricken through as reward-hacked artefacts

**Reward Hacking Analysis (Table 3):**

| Model | Mean Response Len | Mean Explanation Len |
|---|---|---|
| Qwen3-32B-Base-ScalarRM-GRPO | 1872 | 417 |
| **Writing-Zero (GenRM-BRPO)** | **1292** | **58** |
| Writing-SFT | 1251 | 125 |

The ScalarRM-GRPO model generates 417 tokens of redundant self-justification per response on average; Writing-Zero generates only 58 — a **7x reduction** in reward hacking behaviour.

**Human Evaluation:** On 166 test instances, Writing-R1 vs. Writing-SFT: 47 wins, 106 ties, 13 losses (strongly in favour). Writing-R1 vs. Writing-SFT-ScalarRM-GRPO: 28 wins, 120 ties, 18 losses (also favoured).

**Test-Time Scaling:** Majority voting with the GenRM across n=1,2,4,8 calls improves accuracy from 57.5% to 59.2% (Cultural & Creative Writing) and from 54.8% to 57.6% (Lifestyle Copywriting) at n=8. Writing-Zero with voting@2 further improves WritingBench to 8.35 and Writing Testset to 3.86.

---

### 6. Limitations

Explicitly acknowledged:

1. **Computational bottleneck in GenRM training:** Dynamic sampling caused the training data drop rate to exceed 95% in later stages, forcing premature halting of GenRM training. The model used in RL is therefore likely sub-optimal; further refinement could improve results.

2. **Test-time scaling not fully explored:** Due to limited compute, the trends for majority voting (n > 2) during RL training were not systematically studied.

3. **Hyperparameter analysis incomplete:** The impact of various settings in both GenRM and RL stages was not fully investigated.

4. **No unified multi-paradigm framework yet:** Integration of rule-based, reference-based, and reference-free reward modelling into a single unified framework is left as future work.

5. **No multimodal extension:** The work is text-only; multimodal scenarios are flagged as future work.

6. **Position bias in GenRM:** The cold-started GenRM exhibited strong position bias (preferring latter responses). This was partially addressed through RL training and a dynamic advantage weighting mechanism, but variance reduction remained challenging.

7. **Primarily Chinese:** Training and evaluation are predominantly in Chinese, which may limit direct generalisability to English-language writing tasks.

---

### 7. Code/Models

The paper is from Quark LLM at Alibaba Group. **No public code repository or model release is mentioned anywhere in the paper.** All datasets (preference data, Writing Testset), the GenRM, and the writing models (Writing-Zero, Writing-R1) appear to be **internal/proprietary**. The training framework is built on the open-source **verl** (Sheng et al., 2024) and uses **vLLM** for inference.

## Comprehensive Summary: "Self-Rewarding Language Models" (arXiv:2401.10020)

**Authors:** Weizhe Yuan, Richard Yuanzhe Pang, Kyunghyun Cho, Xian Li, Sainbayar Sukhbaatar, Jing Xu, Jason Weston (Meta / NYU)

---

### 1. Research Question

The paper addresses a fundamental bottleneck in LLM alignment: current RLHF methods rely on a **frozen reward model trained from human preferences**, which caps model quality at human performance level and cannot improve during LLM training. The paper asks: can a single language model simultaneously act as both the instruction-following agent and its own reward model, and can both capabilities improve together through iterative self-training — without requiring additional human annotations beyond a small seed set?

---

### 2. Key Methodology: The Self-Rewarding Mechanism and Training Loop

The core idea is that one model plays two roles at once: response generator and judge of its own outputs.

**Two seed data types are used for initialization (M1):**
- **IFT (Instruction Fine-Tuning) data**: 3,200 human-authored (instruction, response) pairs from Open Assistant, used for standard supervised fine-tuning.
- **EFT (Evaluation Fine-Tuning) data**: 1,630 training examples of how to act as an LLM-as-a-Judge. Each example is an (evaluation prompt, chain-of-thought justification + score out of 5) pair, constructed from Open Assistant's ranked human responses. This teaches the model to score responses using an **additive 5-point rubric** covering relevance, coverage, usefulness, clarity, and expertise.

**The iterative training loop (per iteration):**

1. **Generate new prompts**: New instruction prompts are produced via few-shot prompting (Self-Instruct style), sampling from IFT seed data.
2. **Generate candidate responses**: The current model generates N=4 diverse candidate responses per prompt (temperature sampling).
3. **Self-evaluate (LLM-as-a-Judge)**: The same model evaluates each candidate response using the 5-point rubric prompt (run 3 times, scores averaged to reduce variance).
4. **Construct preference pairs**: The highest-scoring and lowest-scoring responses form a (winner, loser) preference pair. Pairs where scores are equal are discarded.
5. **DPO training**: Preference pairs are used to train the next iteration model via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO).

**Model sequence:**
- M0: Base pretrained Llama 2 70B (no fine-tuning)
- M1: M0 + SFT on IFT+EFT seed data
- M2: M1 + DPO on AIFT(M1) — 3,964 preference pairs
- M3: M2 + DPO on AIFT(M2) — 6,942 preference pairs

The key insight is that as the model improves at instruction following, it also improves at judging responses — so the quality of the preference data generated for each successive iteration increases, creating a virtuous cycle.

---

### 3. Datasets Used

**Training data:**
- **Open Assistant** (Köpf et al., 2023): Provides both IFT seed examples (3,200 highest-rank English first-turn conversations) and EFT data (1,630 train / 541 eval examples derived from ranked multi-response prompts).
- **AIFT data** (self-generated): 3,964 preference pairs for M2; 6,942 for M3.

**Evaluation benchmarks:**
- **AlpacaEval 2.0**: 805 prompts, win rate vs. GPT-4 Turbo judged by GPT-4. Primary leaderboard metric.
- **IFT test set**: 256 prompts (head-to-head GPT-4 evaluation, also replicated with human authors).
- **MT-Bench** (Zheng et al., 2023): Multi-turn benchmark across 8 categories (writing, roleplay, reasoning, math, coding, extraction, STEM, humanities), graded 0-10 by GPT-4.
- **9 NLP benchmarks**: ARC-Easy, ARC-Challenge, HellaSwag, SIQA, PIQA, GSM8K, MMLU, OBQA, NQ.
- **Reward modeling eval set**: 541 examples from Open Assistant with human preference rankings; measured via pairwise accuracy, exact match, Spearman correlation, Kendall's tau, and "5-best %" (whether the model's top-scored response matches human top rank).

---

### 4. Baselines

- **SFT Baseline**: Llama 2 70B fine-tuned only on 3,200 IFT examples (no self-rewarding loop).
- **Llama 2 70B base model**: No fine-tuning.
- **AlpacaEval 2.0 leaderboard comparisons** (for context): GPT-4 0314 (22.07%), Mistral Medium (21.86%), Claude 2 (17.19%), Gemini Pro (16.85%), GPT-4 0613 (15.76%), GPT-3.5 Turbo (14.13%), LLaMA2 Chat 70B (13.87%), Vicuna 33B v1.3 (12.71%), Humpback LLaMA2 70B (10.12%).
- **Ablations**: IFT-only self-rewarding loop (no EFT data), SFT augmentation with positive examples only (no preference pairs / DPO).

---

### 5. Key Results

**Instruction following (head-to-head vs. SFT Baseline, GPT-4 judge):**
- M1 vs. SFT Baseline: essentially tied (30.5% vs. 30.9%)
- M2 vs. SFT Baseline: 49.2% wins vs. 14.5%
- M3 vs. SFT Baseline: 62.5% wins vs. 9.8%
- M3 vs. M1: 68.8% wins vs. 8.6% — a very large margin

**AlpacaEval 2.0 win rates (vs. GPT-4 Turbo):**
- M1: 9.94%
- M2: 15.38%
- M3: 20.44% — surpassing Claude 2 (17.19%), Gemini Pro (16.85%), and GPT-4 0613 (15.76%), using only a small Open Assistant seed set and no distillation from stronger models.

**Reward modeling ability (pairwise accuracy vs. human rankings):**
- SFT Baseline (IFT only): 65.1%
- M1 (IFT+EFT): 78.7%
- M2: 80.4%
- M3: 81.7%
- Spearman correlation improves from 0.253 (SFT) to 0.349 (M3).

**MT-Bench overall score:**
- SFT Baseline: 6.85
- M1: 6.78 (slight dip)
- M2: 7.01
- M3: 7.25
- Largest gains in writing (8.83 → 9.58), roleplay (8.15 → 8.73), extraction (6.90 → 7.80), STEM (9.18 → 9.45). Smallest gains in math and coding.

**Human evaluation (50 prompts, 3 annotators per pair, majority vote):**
- M1 vs. SFT: 28% wins vs. 46% (M1 actually worse)
- M2 vs. SFT: 56% wins vs. 20%
- M3 vs. SFT: 66% wins vs. 18%
- Confirms the trend seen in automatic evaluation.

**NLP benchmarks**: Performance largely maintained vs. baseline; some slight degradation (e.g., ARC-Challenge drops from ~57% to ~53% by M3), consistent with the known "alignment tax" and the fact that Open Assistant prompts are not domain-matched to these tasks.

**Key ablation findings:**
- EFT data is critical: without it, only 541 valid DPO pairs could be collected (vs. 3,964 with EFT) because the model without EFT training collapses all scores to 4.
- Preference pairs (DPO) are essential: adding only positive (score=5) examples via SFT augmentation showed zero improvement (29% vs. 30% vs. SFT baseline).
- The additive 5-point rubric prompt (their design) dramatically outperforms the multiple-choice rubric from Li et al. [2024]: 65.1% vs. 26.6% pairwise accuracy.

---

### 6. Limitations

The authors explicitly flag several limitations:

1. **Preliminary results**: Only 3 iterations tested in one model-size/seed-data configuration. Scaling laws across more iterations or different base models are unknown.
2. **Length bias**: Response length increases with each iteration (M1: avg 1,092 tokens, M2: 1,552, M3: 2,552). There is a known correlation between length and judged quality that conflates actual quality improvement with verbosity.
3. **Reward hacking risk**: Since both the training reward and the evaluation judge (GPT-4) are LLMs, there is risk of reward hacking that needs deeper analysis.
4. **No safety evaluation**: No safety-specific analysis was conducted. The authors acknowledge this is an important open problem and suggest that the LLM-as-a-Judge mechanism could in principle be applied to safety criteria.
5. **Saturation**: Improvement likely saturates; the mechanism for when and why is not yet understood.
6. **Math and reasoning**: The method does not significantly improve math, code, or reasoning tasks, likely because the Open Assistant seed data underemphasizes these domains.
7. **Human evaluation scope**: The human evaluation was limited to 50 prompts, evaluated by the authors themselves (not independent annotators).

---

### 7. Code / Models

The paper does **not mention** releasing code or model weights. It is a Meta/NYU research paper published on arXiv (arXiv:2401.10020v3, last updated March 28, 2025). No GitHub repository or Hugging Face model release is referenced in the text.

---

### 8. Relevance to Using Self-Critique for Improving Story Writing Quality

This paper is highly relevant to the research question of whether self-critique can improve AI story writing. Several direct connections:

**Supportive evidence:**
- The paper proves the core principle: a model can evaluate its own outputs well enough that those evaluations, when used as training signal, genuinely improve the model's output quality — measurably and progressively across iterations.
- The MT-Bench fine-grained results are particularly relevant: the **Writing** category shows the strongest gains across iterations (8.83 → 9.10 → 9.10 → 9.58 out of 10), as does **Roleplay** (8.15 → 7.65 → 8.00 → 8.73). These are the most story-adjacent categories in the benchmark. This suggests self-rewarding mechanisms may be especially effective for creative/generative tasks.
- The LLM-as-a-Judge prompt design matters enormously for quality domains. The additive rubric (evaluating multiple distinct quality axes: relevance, coverage, usefulness, clarity, expertise) dramatically outperforms a holistic scale. For story writing, one could design analogous rubrics covering narrative criteria (plot coherence, character voice, pacing, prose style, emotional impact).

**Important caveats and open questions:**
- The method works by learning to distinguish better from worse responses — but it depends on the model already having variance in output quality and the ability to rank that variance accurately. For story writing, subjective quality is harder to ground: a 5-point rubric may be harder to calibrate than for factual instruction-following tasks.
- Length inflation is a major concern for story writing, where longer is not inherently better and the model may learn to pad rather than improve.
- The gains are primarily in instruction-following fluency and informativeness, not in deep creative originality. Whether self-critique can catch and correct failures of craft (weak metaphors, flat characters, predictable arcs) remains untested.
- The paper shows the mechanism improves what the model already knows how to do; it does not inject genuinely new creative knowledge. For story writing quality, this means self-critique may hit a ceiling set by the model's existing narrative understanding.
- The preference pair approach (best vs. worst of N=4 samples) maps cleanly onto a story writing pipeline: generate multiple drafts, have the model judge them, train on the contrast. This is a directly actionable methodology.

**Bottom line for the research project:** This paper provides strong theoretical and empirical grounding that iterative self-evaluation can improve open-ended generative quality — including writing tasks — without human feedback at inference time. The framework is directly adaptable: generate multiple story drafts, apply a domain-specific rubric (craft-focused rather than informativeness-focused), construct preference pairs, and run DPO iterations. The critical design decisions to get right are the evaluation rubric structure (additive, multi-criteria, specific to narrative quality) and the training signal format (preference pairs, not positive-only filtering).

## Comprehensive Summary: "Collective Critics for Creative Story Generation" (arXiv:2410.02428)

**Authors:** Minwook Bae and Hyounghun Kim, Artificial Intelligence Graduate School, UNIST
**Published:** October 3, 2024 (EMNLP 2024)

---

### 1. Research Question

The paper addresses a core deficiency in existing long-form story generation frameworks: they achieve narrative coherence but systematically fail at **creativity**. Specifically, prior plan-then-generate pipelines (e.g., DOC, Re3) produce coherent but generic stories that lack originality in story structure, emotional flow, unusual endings, flashback techniques, and vivid sentence-level expressiveness. The research question is: how can a collaborative critique mechanism be integrated into the plan-to-story pipeline to enhance both creativity and expressiveness without sacrificing coherence?

---

### 2. Key Methodology: How the "Collective Critics" Work

The CRITICS framework has two sequential stages, each driven by a multi-agent critique loop:

#### Stage 1: CRPLAN (Plan Refinement)
- **Input:** A story premise. An initial story plan is generated using the DOC pipeline.
- **Three critics** each evaluate the plan under one of three distinct creativity criteria:
  - "Original theme and background setting" (unconventional themes, unique plots, diverse settings)
  - "Unusual story structure" (non-linear timelines, flashbacks, metafiction, intertextuality)
  - "Unusual ending" (unexpected conclusions, humorous/witty endings, provocative statements)
- Each critic is assigned an **adaptive persona** automatically generated from the story's themes (e.g., for a dystopian emotion story: Sociologist, Psychologist specializing in emotions, Futurist). Personas anchor critiques to the specific narrative, preventing generic or off-topic suggestions.
- Each critic produces three candidate questions/suggestions, selects their best one internally, then passes it to a **Leader**.
- The **Leader** (embodied as a literature editor or creative writing expert) evaluates all three critics' suggestions against the criteria of Originality, Coherence, and Interestingness, then selects the single best critique.
- The selected critique is applied to **refine the story plan**.
- This loop runs for **I rounds** (set to 3 in experiments), generating a list of candidate plans.
- An **Evaluator** (GPT-4 at temperature=0, with randomized ordering to reduce bias) reviews all candidate plans and selects the final one that best balances creativity with coherence.

#### Stage 2: CRTEXT (Story Text Refinement)
- **Input:** The long story generated from the CRPLAN output.
- **Two critics** evaluate individual sentences under two expressiveness criteria:
  - **Image:** Vivid mental imagery — visual, auditory, sensory, emotional language (e.g., "soft glow emitted from the moon," "buzzing market")
  - **Voice:** Unique, recognizable writing style — informal language, unusual word choices, distinctive sentence structures, authenticity (e.g., colloquialisms, comic onomatopoeia)
- Critics have **no personas** in CRTEXT because the Image and Voice criteria already provide sufficiently specific guidance; personas are not needed to anchor style.
- There is **no evaluator** in CRTEXT because each sentence round refines a different randomly sampled sentence; there is no need for cross-round plan selection.
- The **Leader** selects the better of the two critiques (Image vs. Voice) based on which is more contextually appropriate (e.g., for dialogue, Voice critiques tend to be more suitable).
- The selected critique is applied to refine that sentence. The process runs for 3 rounds, each on a new randomly selected sentence.

#### Human-Machine Collaboration
The framework explicitly supports human writers stepping into any role — as a critic, providing or modifying critiques, or as the leader, selecting which critique to apply. A web application was built to demonstrate this.

---

### 3. Datasets Used

No novel dataset is introduced. The paper uses:

- **300 premises from the DOC dataset** (Yang et al., 2023) as story seeds for all main experiments. These premises are short scenario descriptions (e.g., "A high school junior struggling with mental health finds an envelope with $500 cash...").
- The same 300 premises are used for both CRPLAN evaluation (comparing story plans) and CRTEXT evaluation (comparing story text).
- For streamlined in-house evaluations of baselines and ablations, **10 stories per experiment** are used (not a named external benchmark).

There is no standard held-out test set from an existing benchmark; evaluation is entirely pairwise human comparison.

---

### 4. Baselines

- **DOC (Yang et al., 2023)** — "Improving Long Story Coherence with Detailed Outline Control." The primary baseline. Uses a 2-layer hierarchical outline and ChatGPT (GPT-3.5-turbo) backbone. Used for 300-story main experiments.
- **Re3 (Yang et al., 2022)** — "Generating Longer Stories with Recursive Reprompting and Revision." Used in streamlined 10-story in-house evaluation to confirm the framework generalizes across baselines.
- **DOC with GPT-4 backbone** — Also tested in a streamlined 10-story evaluation to confirm the framework generalizes across LLM backbones.

All comparisons are pairwise: human annotators (or GPT-4 auto-eval) are shown two outputs and asked which is better.

---

### 5. Key Results

#### CRPLAN vs. DOC (300 stories, human evaluation — Table 3):
| Metric | DOC | CRPLAN |
|---|---|---|
| Interesting | 57.56% | **85.00%** |
| Coherent | 67.33% | **77.89%** |
| Creative | 57.33% | **84.33%** |
| Relevant | 95.11% | 96.00% |

CRPLAN wins by large margins on Interesting (+27.4pp) and Creative (+27pp), and also improves Coherence (+10.6pp). Relevance is near-equal. Statistical significance: p < 0.05 for bolded results.

#### CRTEXT vs. DOC (300 stories, human evaluation — Table 4):
| Metric | DOC | CRTEXT |
|---|---|---|
| Interesting | 69.89% | **80.00%** |
| Coherence | 69.11% | 71.89% |
| Consistency | 76.89% | 80.00% |
| Creative | 70.67% | **89.33%** |

CRTEXT wins significantly on Interesting (+10.1pp) and Creative (+18.7pp), with slight gains on Coherence and Consistency.

#### Ablation findings:
- **Multiple criteria vs. single criterion:** Three-criteria critiques produce structurally diverse plans (flashbacks, new themes, character development) vs. single-criterion critiques that only adjust one dimension (e.g., ethical dilemmas). Significant qualitative difference confirmed.
- **Leader vs. no leader:** Without the leader, narrative contradictions emerge (e.g., a character who disappeared inexplicably reappears). The leader ensures coherent integration of critiques.
- **Persona vs. no persona:** Without personas, critiques are generic and often off-topic (e.g., suggesting parallel universes for a story about energy research). With personas, critiques are specific and story-relevant. GPT-4 auto-eval on 100 pairs shows personas improve all metrics (Interesting: 85% vs. 80%, Coherent: 95% vs. 70%, Creative: 65% vs. 60%).

#### Against Re3 baseline (10-story in-house evaluation — Table 21):
CRPLAN: Creativity 65% vs. Re3's 10% (massive gap). Coherence: 85% vs. 70%.
CRTEXT: Interesting 60% vs. Re3's 40%. Creative 78.3% vs. 65%.

#### Against DOC (GPT-4 backbone, 10 stories — Table 22):
CRPLAN: Creative 100% vs. DOC-GPT4's 65%. Coherent 95% vs. 80%.
CRTEXT: Interesting 91.7% vs. 71.7%. Creative 93.3% vs. 58.3%.

#### Number of rounds analysis (Figure 3):
There is a clear **creativity-coherence trade-off** across rounds. Creativity increases with more rounds; coherence decreases. This is why the Evaluator module is essential — it selects the best round rather than always using the final one.

#### Human-machine interactive writing (Table 8):
In a 30-story user study (3 annotators × 10 stories, 3 rounds each): 100% of stories changed in response to critiques (Edited pass rate), and 83.33% aligned with user-specified critique content (Accepted pass rate). The gap indicates the LLM does not always faithfully implement the exact content of human-provided critiques.

---

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Human evaluation** is the primary method. Three professional annotators evaluate pairwise comparisons with a structured questionnaire. Statistical significance verified via t-test. Inter-annotator agreement measured with Fleiss' Kappa (mostly fair to moderate, κ ≈ 0.2–0.5).

**For CRPLAN:**
- **Interesting:** How well the plan engages and captivates readers
- **Coherent:** Logical organization and interconnection of narrative elements
- **Relevant:** Adherence of narrative themes to the given premise
- **Creative:** Originality and inventiveness vs. typical narratives

**For CRTEXT:**
- **Interesting:** Richness and engagement of sentence expressions
- **Coherence:** Causal and temporal sentence-level dependencies
- **Writing Style Consistency:** Whether refined sentences match the surrounding story's style
- **Creative:** Unique or rich expression different from typical story prose

**Automatic evaluation (supplementary):** GPT-4 at temperature=0 is used for ablation studies on 100 story plan pairs, following Wang et al. (2022a)'s pairwise evaluation protocol. Story order randomized to reduce position bias. Cohen's Kappa between GPT-4 and each human annotator shows fair-to-moderate agreement (κ ≈ 0.17–0.62), validating GPT-4 as a reliable supplementary evaluator. (Lower kappa for "Relevant" is explained by data bias — both methods tend to score high, producing ceiling effects.)

The creativity metrics are grounded in **Mozaffari (2013)'s rubric** for assessing creative writing, which defines creativity along three dimensions: Image, Voice, and Originality.

---

### 7. Code and Models

**Code is publicly released:**
`https://github.com/EMNLP-2024-CritiCS/Collective-Critics-for-Creative-Story-Generation`

The paper uses off-the-shelf models (no custom fine-tuning):
- **ChatGPT (GPT-3.5-turbo):** Used for all critics, leaders, and story generation (temperature=1 for critics and leaders in both stages)
- **GPT-4:** Used for the CRPLAN evaluator (temperature=0) and for automatic evaluation experiments
- The DOC pipeline's official implementation is used as the story generation backbone

No novel model weights are released.

---

### 8. Relevance to Self-Critique for Story Writing

This paper is directly and centrally relevant to the research question "Is self-critique enough to get good AI story writers?" — and its findings provide a nuanced, empirically grounded answer:

**Self-critique is not enough; collective critique is needed.** The paper's core argument is that a *single* LLM critiquing its own output is insufficient for creative story generation. Several design choices in CRITICS are specifically motivated by the limitations of self-critique:

1. **Multiple critics with diverse criteria:** A single critic with a single criterion produces narrow improvements (e.g., only adjusting ethical themes). Three critics with three different creativity criteria produce structurally richer changes (flashbacks, new themes, character arcs). Self-critique by one model under one lens is inherently limited in scope.

2. **Personas to prevent generic feedback:** Without personas, a single LLM asked to critique a story generates broad, often off-topic suggestions (e.g., "add a parallel universe"). Personas ground the critique in story-specific expertise, simulating the diversity of knowledge that a real collaborative writing group would bring.

3. **A leader to resolve conflicts and select appropriately:** Without a selecting agent, applying all critiques simultaneously produces incoherent results (e.g., applying Image critique to dialogue when Voice critique is more appropriate). The leader's role models the editorial judgment that is absent in naive self-critique loops.

4. **An evaluator to manage the creativity-coherence trade-off:** More rounds of self-revision increase creativity but degrade coherence — a finding directly relevant to the project. A naive "iterate and take the last output" approach would not work; an evaluator selecting the best round is necessary.

5. **The Huang et al. (2023a) citation is telling:** The paper explicitly cites work showing "large language models cannot self-correct reasoning yet" as motivation for moving beyond self-critique to multi-agent critique with external feedback. This positions CRITICS as a deliberate architectural response to the failure mode of self-critique.

**Key takeaway for the research project:** The CRITICS paper demonstrates that iterative critique improves story creativity significantly (up to +27pp on creativity and interestingness metrics) but that the quality of that critique depends critically on diversity (multiple criteria, multiple personas) and editorial control (leader, evaluator). Naive self-critique — one model critiquing its own story under a single criterion — is likely insufficient. The paper provides a strong empirical baseline and a clear architecture to compare against.

2. papers/2212.08073_constitutional_ai.pdf (read pages 1-3) - Constitutional AI

8. papers/2309.00267_rlaif_vs_rlhf.pdf (read pages 1-3) - RLAIF vs RLHF

For each paper, provide a concise summary (3-5 sentences) covering: what it does, key methodology, main results, and relevance to "using LLM self-critique as reward for improving story writing."

Papers to skim (read chunk 001 only):
1. papers/pages/2303.17651_self_refine_chunk_001.pdf - Self-Refine
2. papers/2212.08073_constitutional_ai.pdf (read pages 1-3) - Constitutional AI
3. papers/2210.11610_llm_can_self_improve.pdf (read pages 1-3) - LLMs Can Self-Improve
4. papers/2305.14483_lm_self_improvement_rl_contemplation.pdf (read pages 1-3) - RL Contemplation
5. papers/2407.04549_spontaneous_reward_hacking.pdf (read pages 1-3) - Spontaneous Reward Hacking
6. papers/2411.00418_self_evolved_reward_learning.pdf (read pages 1-3) - Self-Evolved Reward
7. papers/2501.12948_no_free_lunch_internal_feedback.pdf (read pages 1-3) - No Free Lunch
8. papers/2309.00267_rlaif_vs_rlhf.pdf (read pages 1-3) - RLAIF vs RLHF
9. papers/2312.03746_evaluating_llm_creativity_literary.pdf (read pages 1-3) - Evaluating LLM Creativity
10. papers/2406.18476_small_but_funny_humor.pdf (read pages 1-3) - Small But Funny
11. papers/2508.06046_evolvr_story_evaluation.pdf (read pages 1-3) - EvolvR
12. papers/2510.21721_prefine_personalized_story.pdf (read pages 1-3) - PREFINE
13. papers/2407.17511_art_of_story_evaluation_llm.pdf (read pages 1-3) - Art of Story Evaluation
14. papers/2305.18290_dpo_direct_preference_optimization.pdf (read pages 1-3) - DPO
15. papers/2306.05685_judging_llm_as_judge.pdf (read pages 1-3) - Judging LLM-as-a-Judge

For each, give: Title, Authors (first author + year), Key idea, Main result, Relevance to our topic.
